metadata.from: James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) — signed 'Love Dad,' written on Hanifen Body & Paint Co. letterhead, consistent with the father writing to his son John
metadata.location_sent_to: Col. John R. Hanifen, Co. C 439th Eng Con Bn, APO 301, San Francisco, California
metadata.envelope_notes: 6-cent Air Mail stamp. Written on Hanifen Body & Paint Co. envelope, 801 Park Street, Des Moines 14, Iowa. Handwritten annotation on front: 'to Bob' with an arrow — possibly indicating this letter was later passed to Bob or filed with Bob's collection.
metadata.mood_tone: Warm, casual, slightly scolding but affectionate; father checking in with deployed son, sharing home-front news with humor
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but somewhat hurried and slanted; some words require inference from context. Page 2 has show-through from the reverse side making some passages harder to read.
metadata.historical_context: Written during the Korean War era; John is serving with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion. References the 1952 Republican National Convention and Eisenhower's nomination, as well as the ongoing steel strike of 1952.

pages.transcription:
July 12 - 1952

Dear John

Just a few lines to give you the Weather Data & so forth. This is the Day, The Republicans & Eisenhower are Shouting. I don't No, but what, he might Make it, I supose, all you guys will Vote for him. It is a little quiet here You No a Big Steel Strike is still on.

Do you hear from Bob — I hear Moone often from you. Some one Must have Said Something Jimmie Bought a Nice white Sport Shirt for you on Father day. Better write heem & let him No, it was ok a Box of Cigars from Jim a Shirt from you & Not even a card from Bob doing pretty good don't you think.

Sat morning 7:45 AM

Des Moines 14, Iowa, July 12 - 52

Not the First Page

IN ACCOUNT WITH
HANIFEN BODY & PAINT CO.
J. E. Hanifen
We Specialize in Repairing Wrecked Cars
Phone 4-8223          801 Park Street          Res. Phone 4-9704
All bills due and payable the 10th of the month following date of purchase

Say John, did we ever Send you a card or Picture of your Car. It is worth about 18⁰⁰ and if the Strike Keeps up, it may go to 2000 John Hold on to your dough, it is hard to ge a hold of here. The dif guys stop & holler where you are Keeping your Self. The Taylor Boys & Godard Thought you Would Be here for Sure. By Now Well So long for Now If you got any Bitching to do, Put it in the Letter     Love Dad

full_transcription:
July 12 - 1952

Dear John

Just a few lines to give you the Weather Data & so forth. This is the Day, The Republicans & Eisenhower are Shouting. I don't No, but what, he might Make it, I supose, all you guys will Vote for him. It is a little quiet here You No a Big Steel Strike is still on.

Do you hear from Bob — I hear Moone often from you. Some one Must have Said Something Jimmie Bought a Nice white Sport Shirt for you on Father day. Better write heem & let him No, it was ok a Box of Cigars from Jim a Shirt from you & Not even a card from Bob doing pretty good don't you think.

---

Sat morning 7:45 AM

Des Moines 14, Iowa, July 12 - 52

Not the First Page

Say John, did we ever Send you a card or Picture of your Car. It is worth about 18⁰⁰ and if the Strike Keeps up, it may go to 2000 John Hold on to your dough, it is hard to ge a hold of here. The dif guys stop & holler where you are Keeping your Self. The Taylor Boys & Godard Thought you Would Be here for Sure. By Now Well So long for Now If you got any Bitching to do, Put it in the Letter     Love Dad
summary: Written July 12, 1952 by James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) to his son Col. John R. Hanifen, who is serving with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion in Korea (via APO San Francisco). The father shares home-front news including the Republican National Convention and Eisenhower's apparent nomination, the ongoing steel strike, and notes about Father's Day gifts — Jimmie bought John a white sport shirt, but Bob sent nothing, not even a card. Dad advises John to hold onto his money as a car John owns has risen in value due to the strike, and closes warmly telling John to put any complaints in his letters.
